name: Guka
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Trifon Andreev, Vika Usmanova
classification_notes: Guka is rooted in the Clarendon and Ionic slab serif tradition, distinguished by thick geometrical terminals in place of classic Clarendon drops, giving it an industrial character rather than a Victorian one.
summary: A Clarendon-inspired slab serif with thick geometrical terminals and an industrial character, available in nine weights with matching italics and variable fonts.
description: Drawing on the Clarendon and Ionic tradition, Guka replaces the genre's classic drops with thick geometric terminals, most visible in the C, G and S, while horizontal endings on the f and j reinforce its sculptural, industrial tone. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus variable font files for both upright and italic axes. Intended for branding, packaging, editorial and poster work where a slab needs personality without looking historically pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Guka as a contemporary reinterpretation of classical Clarendon and Ionic-inspired typefaces with a stronger industrial character and a more modern attitude. They highlight its thick geometrical terminals, distinctive f and j forms, and systematic consistency across 18 styles, stylistic sets and multilingual support, positioning it for branding, editorial design, packaging and visual identity systems.

name: Fabric Grotesk
designer: Ani Dimitrova, Vika Usmanova, Leon Hugues, Kiril Zlatkov
classification_notes: Fontfabric explicitly classify it as Neo-Grotesk, citing the Swiss and International Graphic Style as its primary inspiration; clean horizontal and vertical terminals with uniform construction place it firmly in the Helvetica lineage.
summary: A neo-grotesque from Fontfabric drawing on the Swiss International Style, offered in 18 static styles plus a variable font with Extended Latin and Cyrillic support.
description: Inspired by the visual clarity of Armin Hofmann and Josef Müller-Brockmann, Fabric Grotesk uses clean horizontal and vertical terminals with carefully balanced curves across nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. A display-oriented stylistic set shifts the character from quietly functional body copy to something sharper and more angular in headlines, and Bulgarian Cyrillic forms are included alongside a full OpenType feature set. It positions itself as a general-purpose workhorse for branding, editorial, screen and packaging work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Fabric Grotesk as their take on the Neo-Grotesk tradition that defined mid-century modern design, citing the Swiss and International Graphic Style and masters such as Armin Hofmann and Josef Müller-Brockmann as inspiration. They emphasise its versatility across brand systems, editorial layouts, interfaces and packaging, and highlight the included Bulgarian Cyrillic forms, rich alternates, display-oriented stylistic set, and variable font as features for serious design work across print and screen.

name: Nexa Slab
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Ivelina Martinova, Ani Dimitrova
classification_notes: Nexa Slab is a geometric slab serif with square proportions, low stroke contrast and rounded counters, drawing directly from the tradition of Rockwell, Lubalin Graph and Memphis.
summary: A geometric slab companion to Fontfabric's Nexa, offering 27 styles across upright, italic and oblique cuts with variable font support and extended Cyrillic.
description: Built as a companion to the Nexa sans-serif, this typeface takes its cues from geometric slabs like Rockwell, Lubalin Graph and Memphis, pairing square proportions and low stroke contrast with rounded counters that soften the overall texture. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Black, each available in upright, italic and oblique, with variable TTF versions also included. A recent update added an extended Cyrillic character set, refined kerning and additional OpenType features including small capitals, fractions and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Nexa Slab as a robust companion to their popular Nexa, inspired by classic geometric slab serifs including Rockwell, Lubalin Graph and Memphis. They highlight its precise square proportions, low stroke contrast and friendly rounded counters as giving it both confidence and approachability, and note that a recent update expanded Cyrillic coverage, refined the Latin glyph set and introduced additional OpenType features for modern typographic needs.

name: Panton Arabic
designer: Leo Guibert, Nadine Chahine (consulting), Plamen Motev
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans with softened construction; the Arabic extension matches the visual language of the Latin/Cyrillic Panton family through a shared rounded geometric structure.
summary: An Arabic extension of the Panton family, sharing its softened geometric construction across Latin, Cyrillic and Arabic scripts in nine static weights plus a variable font.
description: Designed by Leo Guibert with consulting from type specialist Nadine Chahine, Panton Arabic extends the Fontfabric bestseller into Arabic script while preserving the rounded geometric character of the original. The family covers nine static weights from Thin to Heavy alongside a variable font, with broad multilingual support across Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Arabic. OpenType features include alternates, stylistic sets, ligatures and localised forms, making it suited to brand systems and interfaces that must perform consistently across multiple scripts.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Panton Arabic as bringing the signature spirit of Panton into Arabic script — geometric, confident, and shaped with a modern, tech-friendly feel. Designed by Leo Guibert with consulting support from Nadine Chahine, it is built for strong headlines, polished identities, and flexible design systems that need to perform across Latin, Cyrillic and Arabic. Released in March 2026, the family comprises nine static styles plus a variable font, with advanced OpenType features and expanded multilingual support.

name: Postamp Grotesk
classification_notes: A condensed geometric sans with grotesk characteristics, drawing from early American type specimens and stamp lettering. The variable width axis and selectively expanded letterforms place it firmly in the geometric-grotesk tradition.
summary: A condensed geometric sans drawn from early American stamp lettering, with a variable width axis that selectively widens individual letterforms as visual accents within condensed text.
description: Designed by Ivelina Martinova and released in January 2026, the family takes its starting point from a U.S. postage stamp bearing President James Buchanan's portrait and expands outward through early American type specimens from the 1920s and 30s. Five weights run in two cuts each: a fully condensed version and a Mixed cut where letters such as B, C, G, M, O and R carry expanded widths alongside otherwise condensed forms. A variable font covers both weight and width axes, and the character set includes Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Postamp Grotesk as a modern condensed sans serif blending Grotesk and Geometric traditions with a refined vintage spirit, inspired by American typography from the late 19th and early 20th centuries and the lettering found on historic U.S. postage stamps. The defining feature is the integration of selectively expanded letterforms acting as visual accents within otherwise condensed text, supported by a variable font with weight and width axes, OpenType features, and Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage.

name: Trixy
classification_notes: A high-contrast condensed serif with sharp vertical serifs and swash details inspired by Soviet-era Cyrillic lettering, firmly in the display category given its narrow proportions, eccentric details and two expressive stylistic subfamilies.
summary: A condensed display serif drawing on Soviet-era Cyrillic book lettering, offered in two stylistic variants with variable font support.
description: Trixy comes in two subfamilies: Stories, with refined ligatures and stylistic alternates, and Tales, which pushes the expression further with sharper details and elongated swash-like legs. Both share narrow proportions, high contrast and vertical serifs rooted in mid-20th century Cyrillic typographic heritage. Ten static styles run from Light to Bold across the two subfamilies, accompanied by variable font versions, with coverage across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Trixy as a condensed serif typeface inspired by bold, experimental Cyrillic lettering from Soviet-era book covers of the mid-20th century. Beginning with Cyrillic and later expanded into Latin, the family offers two distinct styles — Stories for a refined expressive tone, and Tales for sharper, more eccentric detail — suited to packaging, editorial headlines, book covers and digital graphics.

name: Nexa Pro
designer: Svet Simov, Ivelina Martinova, Ani Dimitrova
classification_notes: Nexa Pro is explicitly described as a geometric sans-serif, built on clean geometric structure with low stroke contrast and optically balanced letterforms — consistent hallmarks of the geometric sans genre.
summary: An expanded, variable-capable update to Fontfabric's popular Nexa family, adding Greek, extended Cyrillic, Vietnamese and small caps across 40 styles.
description: The Pro version of Nexa reworks the original's geometric skeleton with refined proportions and updated characters, then extends it with variable font files, small caps, old-style figures and a broader currency set. The family splits into two subfamilies — Nexa Pro for display and general use, and Nexa Text Pro optimised for smaller sizes — each running nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics. Broad script coverage across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese makes it a workable choice for multilingual branding and digital interface work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Nexa Pro as the evolution of one of their most celebrated geometric sans-serif families, redesigned for the needs of global creatives. They highlight its refined curves, expanded multilingual support including extended Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese, and the addition of variable fonts, small caps, old-style figures and advanced OpenType features for professional typographic control across branding, editorial and digital interface applications.

name: Combina
designer: Ani Dimitrova, Viktoria Usmanova, Stefan Yatanski
classification_notes: Combina is classified as display because its core conceit, dynamically alternating between geometric sans and cursive script letterforms within a single setting, is inherently expressive and unsuited to body text. It defies a single subcategory, sitting somewhere between geometric sans and script as a programmed hybrid.
summary: A display family from Fontfabric that programmatically alternates between geometric sans and cursive script letterforms as you type, creating built-in visual rhythm across six weights.
description: Designed by Ani Dimitrova, Viktoria Usmanova and Stefan Yatanski, Combina combines Futura-influenced geometric forms with a romantic script derived in part from American Script in Pettingill & Co.'s 1901 Copper Alloy Type Book. Contextual alternates engine switches between the two styles automatically, producing contrasting texture without manual intervention. Covers Extended Latin and Cyrillic with OpenType features including stylistic sets, ligatures and case-sensitive glyphs, and is available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Combina as a bold typographic experiment mixing geometric sans-serifs in the spirit of Futura and Avenir with a romantic cursive script inspired by early 20th-century specimen books. The typeface is programmed to dynamically alternate between the two styles as you type, creating rhythm and texture suited to logo lockups, packaging and branding. Released May 2025 with six weights, a variable font, extended multilingual support and a fully reprogrammed contextual alternates engine.

name: Mont
classification_notes: Mont is explicitly constructed from circle, square and triangle primitives, with pointed apexes and circular proportions characteristic of geometric sans-serifs in the Futura tradition.
summary: A geometric sans built on circle, square and triangle forms, released by Fontfabric in 2018 and updated in 2025 with variable font support across 22 styles.
description: Constructed from primary geometric shapes, Mont features pointed apexes on letters such as 'M' and 't' set against circular proportions in 'o', 'a' and 'e', giving it a clean, minimal character. The family spans ten weights from Hairline to Black with matching italics, plus two variable font files added in the 2025 update alongside improved screen hinting. Multilingual support covers Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with a full OpenType feature set including alternates, ligatures and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Mont as born from the pursuit of geometric clarity and minimal elegance, built from the basics of circle, square and triangle. The 2025 update extended the family with variable fonts and improved screen rasterisation for sharper rendering and smoother web performance, making it suited to branding, editorial, UI and print across 22 carefully crafted styles.

name: Resist Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif with deep inktraps and angled terminals derived from the Resist Sans family, squarely placing it in the monospaced category with contemporary grotesque influences.
summary: A monospaced sans-serif with deep inktraps and true italics, designed for code editors and technical design work.
description: Built as a monospaced companion to the Resist Sans family, it features deep inktraps and angled terminals that give the letterforms a sharp, contemporary character without sacrificing legibility. Seven weights run from Thin to Bold with matching true italics, plus variable font instances for both upright and italic axes. Broad multilingual coverage and an extensive OpenType feature set make it suitable across coding environments, UX interfaces and editorial projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Resist Mono as a meticulously crafted monospaced sans-serif by Eugene Tantsurin and Anna Remm, drawing from the original Resist Sans family. It features deep inktraps and angled terminals for a bold, contemporary look, with true italics carrying calligraphic influence, 16 styles in total, and broad multilingual support for use in code editors, technical documentation, data visualisation and minimalist editorial branding.

name: Nexa Serif
designer: Kiril Zlatkov, Ani Dimitrova, Ivelina Martinova
classification_notes: Nexa Serif is explicitly designed as a geometric serif companion to the sans-serif Nexa, preserving geometric letter proportions and clean construction while introducing moderate contrast and serif details. This places it squarely in the geometric serif subcategory.
summary: A geometric serif companion to Fontfabric's Nexa sans-serif, sharing its proportions and clean construction across eight weights with italics and variable font options.
description: Designed as a direct counterpart to the bestselling Nexa, this serif family preserves the sans-serif's letter proportions while introducing moderate contrast and refined details. Alternate forms of 'a', 'g' and 'y' echo Nexa's geometric skeleton, making the two families pair naturally. Eighteen styles span Thin through Black with matching italics, supported by Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage, stylistic alternates and ligatures for both body text and display work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Nexa Serif as a serif counterpart to their bestselling Nexa, years in the making, built to maintain Nexa's geometric simplicity while adding a sophisticated, intricate touch. Key letter proportions and contrast are preserved, with special alternate forms of letters like 'a', 'g' and 'y' drawn directly from Nexa's sans-serif foundation. The family ships with functional and stylistic ligatures, variable font files, and support for Extended Latin and Cyrillic, targeting branding, editorial, web and print applications.

name: Nexa Round
classification_notes: A rounded geometric sans derived from Fontfabric's Nexa, with softened terminals applied across a multi-weight family. The geometric construction and circular forms place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A rounded companion to Fontfabric's Nexa, softening its geometric construction across eleven weights from Thin to an Ultra Black Glow variant.
description: Designed by Ivelina Martinova, Nexa Round takes the geometric structure of the original Nexa and introduces rounded terminals throughout, producing a warmer character while retaining the same underlying proportions. The family runs eleven styles, with the first nine weights mirroring Nexa directly for straightforward pairing, and the Ultra and Glow cuts added as display-weight variants exclusive to this family. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage with OpenType ligatures and contextual alternates makes it workable across branding, packaging and digital applications.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Nexa Round as a friendlier companion to their renowned Nexa, designed by Ivelina Martinova to infuse softness into the geometric simplicity of the original. They position it as a versatile family suited to technology, health and wellness, food and beverage, and children's products, and note that its nine shared weights with Nexa make the two families natural partners for cohesive branding and corporate identity work.

name: Brevis
classification_notes: Brevis is classified as display, drawing on early uncial letterforms recast as a bold, single-weight display face. Its calligraphic historic roots and unconventional construction place it firmly in decorative display territory rather than blackletter or script.
summary: A single-weight bold display typeface drawing on early uncial letterforms, released by Fontfabric in 2024.
description: Designed by Stefan Yatanski, Brevis takes its name from the Latin for 'short' and derives its forms from early uncial calligraphy, redrawn for a contemporary visual register. A single Regular weight with OpenType features including localized forms and ligatures covers Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts. Suited to posters, album covers, packaging and headline work where a historically flavoured but unconventional display face is needed.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Brevis as a bold display typeface inspired by early uncial letterforms, combining historic calligraphic roots with a modern visual language. They position it for expressive design work including album covers, editorial layouts, branding and typographic art, noting its OpenType features and extended multilingual support across Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Matt
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Ani Dimitrova, Ivelina Martinova
classification_notes: Matt is a geometric sans-serif with squared ovals, open terminals, and wide proportions, placing it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition, closely related to the Intro family from the same foundry.
summary: A wide-proportioned geometric sans-serif from Fontfabric, evolved from their Intro family, with squared ovals and open terminals aimed at brand and interface work.
description: Released in 2024, Matt carries forward the geometry of Fontfabric's Intro with wider proportions, a high x-height and open terminals that keep letterforms legible at both headline and interface sizes. The family ships as 16 static styles across eight weights with matching italics, plus two variable fonts covering the full weight axis. Extended Latin and Cyrillic support across 130+ languages, alongside a broad OpenType feature set, gives it genuine range for international brand and digital projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Matt as a contemporary sans-serif that evolves from the popular Intro family, amplifying clarity and modernity through wider proportions, a high x-height, squared ovals and open terminals. They position it for branding, web and UI, headlines, packaging, and tech and gaming interfaces, highlighting its 16 static styles plus two variable fonts, 700+ glyphs, and support for 130+ languages.

name: Gonzaga
designer: Kiril Zlatkov, Ivelina Martinova, Viktoria Usmanova
classification_notes: Fontfabric explicitly describe Gonzaga as a contemporary reinterpretation of the classic Clarendon slab-serif, with a bold, steady skeleton and strong editorial tone characteristic of the Clarendon/Egyptian tradition.
summary: A Clarendon-derived slab-serif from Fontfabric designed for headline and editorial work, available in 18 static styles plus two variable fonts.
description: Built around the proportions and bracketed serifs of the Clarendon tradition, Gonzaga modernises the genre with an expressive ligature set, contextual alternates and expert Cyrillic support including Bulgarian local forms. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and two variable fonts cover the full range in a single file. Use cases lean toward poster design, packaging, album covers and magazine headers where a confident slab with personality is needed.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Gonzaga as a contemporary reinterpretation of the classic Clarendon slab-serif, designed by Kiril Zlatkov and released in May 2024. They position it as charismatic and editorially driven, suited to headlines, branding, packaging, music and festival promos, and note its expert Cyrillic support alongside a broad OpenType feature set including ligatures, stylistic sets and small caps.

name: Transforma
designer: Ani Dimitrova, Viktoria Usmanova, Svetoslav Simov
classification_notes: Transforma straddles sans-serif, script and a hybrid Mix subfamily, making display the most accurate primary category. Its high-contrast weights, ink traps and mixed-structure styles position it firmly as a decorative display system rather than a workhorse text face.
summary: A 22-style family from Fontfabric that blends a geometric sans, a handmade script and a hybrid Mix subfamily into one system.
description: Released in March 2024, Transforma spans three distinct structural modes: a nine-weight sans running from Extra Light to Extra Black, a five-weight script, and a five-weight Mix that merges the two. Ink traps and vertical cuts add character at heavier weights, while the variable fonts cover the full expressive range in a single file. Latin Extended and Cyrillic are both supported, broadening its use across international branding and editorial projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Transforma as a typeface built around the concept of 'where typography meets magic', created by founder Svet, Type Director Viktoria Usmanova and Senior Type Designer Ani Dimitrova. They position it as a type system that blends the precision of a sans with the organic charm of a handmade script, culminating in the Mix subfamily, and highlight its 22 styles, advanced OpenType features and multilingual support as tools for playful, dynamic design work.

name: Chopin
classification_notes: Chopin is a low-contrast geometric sans-serif with a distinctive rhythmic axis alternating wide and narrow letterforms, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with expressive proportional variation.
summary: A geometric sans with a rhythmic alternation of wide and narrow proportions, designed by Svetoslav Simov at Fontfabric and released in 2024.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov with Viktoria Usmanova as type director, Chopin draws from vintage poster and book title lettering to produce a sans-serif with an energetic push and pull between wide and narrow letterforms. The family spans nine weights from Hairline to Black with matching italics, plus two variable fonts covering the full Roman and Italic axes. Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage, alongside OpenType features including ligatures, contextual alternates and old-style figures, makes it well suited to editorial titles, packaging and branding work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Chopin as a sans-serif designed for creative needs ranging from headlines to digital interfaces, citing rhythmic contrasts between wide and narrow proportions that give it a bold, musical dynamism. The family totals 18 styles plus two variable fonts, with multilingual support across Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and advanced OpenType features including stylistic alternates, ligatures and case-sensitive forms.

name: Plush
designer: Joaquín Contreras Soto, Viktoria Usmanova, Plamen Motev, Ivelina Martinova, Stefan Yatanski
classification_notes: Plush draws on rounded, bracketed serifs in the Windsor and Cooper tradition, giving it a soft, high-contrast display character rather than a conventional text serif. Its rounded details and gentle curves point firmly toward display and editorial use.
summary: A rounded display serif inspired by Windsor and Cooper, offered in seven weights with italics and variable font support across 102 languages.
description: Taking its cues from the soft, bracketed serifs of Windsor and Cooper, Plush pairs gentle curves with a structured, high-contrast construction suited to branding, editorial layouts and packaging. The family spans 14 static styles from ExtraLight to Black with matching italics, plus two variable fonts covering the full weight range in roman and italic. OpenType features include old-style figures, discretionary ligatures, contextual alternates and case-sensitive forms, with coverage extending to Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Plush as drawing inspiration from classics like Windsor and Cooper, blending traditional elegance with a contemporary sensibility. They highlight its rounded serifs, gentle curves and strong readable structure, positioning it for high-end branding, editorial layouts, web projects and packaging. The family comprises 16 styles with advanced OpenType features and support for over 100 languages.

name: Salina
designer: Ivelina Martinova, Plamen Motev, Viktoria Usmanova
classification_notes: High contrast between thick and thin strokes with sharp bracketed serifs and ball terminals places Salina firmly in the transitional serif tradition, with a contemporary lean toward display and editorial use.
summary: A high-contrast transitional serif from Fontfabric, available in 32 static styles across two sub-families with swash alternates and variable font support.
description: Designed by Ivelina Martinova with creative direction from Plamen Motev and Viktoria Usmanova, Salina pairs dramatic stroke contrast and sharp bracketed serifs with round ball terminals and graceful ovals across seven weights. A dedicated Swashes sub-family adds ornate alternate characters for invitations, luxury branding and fashion applications. Variable font files cover both roman and italic axes, and OpenType features include small caps, old-style figures, contextual swashes and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Salina as a modern, high-contrast transitional serif crafted for refined, visually striking compositions. They highlight its dramatic thick-to-thin contrast, smooth ovals, round ball terminals and sharp bracketed serifs, and point to small caps and swash styles as key versatility features. The full family comprises 32 static styles plus variable fonts, with extensive multilingual support and advanced OpenType capabilities suited to luxury branding, editorial design, high-end advertising and fashion lookbooks.

name: Petrov Sans
designer: Asen Petrov, Stefan Yatanski, Svetoslav Simov
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with consistent contemporary geometry across a broad weight range, designed with low contrast and futuristic character typical of the geometric genre.
summary: A geometric sans from Fontfabric with nine weights, matching italics and a variable font, covering Extended Latin and Cyrillic across 130-plus languages.
description: Designed by Asen Petrov and refined by Stefan Yatanski, this family spans nine upright weights from Thin to Black with nine matching italics and a variable font file. The geometry is consistent and low-contrast throughout, keeping the face legible at small sizes while giving it enough character for headlines and branding work. Extended Latin and Cyrillic support makes it a practical choice for multilingual publishing and interface design.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Petrov Sans as a futuristic geometric sans-serif released in 2023, offering 18 styles plus a variable font with advanced OpenType features and multilingual support covering over 130 languages across Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts. They position it as a versatile geometric sans suited to headlines, editorial layouts, branding, UI design and social media graphics.

name: Auge
designer: Nikolay Petroussenko, Viktoria Usmanova, Plamen Motev
classification_notes: Auge features high stroke contrast and distinctive wedge serifs drawn from insect morphology, placing it firmly in the high-contrast display serif category rather than a conventional transitional or didone tradition.
summary: A high-contrast serif with wedge serifs drawn from insect morphology, originally a 2017 graduation project by Nikolay Petroussenko and later developed into an 18-style family by Fontfabric.
description: Begun as a graduation project by Nikolay Petroussenko, Auge takes its formal cues from insect anatomy, translating sharp micro-structural detail into wedge serifs and pronounced stroke contrast. The family spans eight weights from Light to Extrablack with matching italics, plus variable font files for the Roman and Italic axes. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage is supported alongside OpenType features including ligatures, oldstyle figures and stylistic alternates, making it workable across editorial, packaging and brand identity contexts.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Auge, German for 'eye', as a serif type family that fuses scientific precision with poetic design, drawing on the beauty of the micro-world through sharp details, high contrast and expressive wedge serifs inspired by insect anatomy. Originally a graduation project by Nikolay Petroussenko in 2017, it was developed and released by Fontfabric in 2023 under the direction of Plamen Motev and Viktoria Usmanova, and is positioned for high-impact editorial, brand identity, packaging and web design.

name: Calleo
designer: Plamen Motev, Léon Hugues, Léo Guibert
classification_notes: A modern sans-serif with low contrast and clean construction typical of geometric grotesques, offering two distinct sub-families with contrasting personalities ranging from neutral to expressive.
summary: A geometric sans-serif family in 36 styles plus a variable font, split between a neutral sub-family and a more expressive counterpart called Flux.
description: Conceived by Plamen Motev in 2017 and completed with French designers Léon Hugues and Léo Guibert, Calleo launched in March 2023. The family divides into two sub-families: Calleo, which offers clean, restrained forms across nine weights, and Calleo Flux, which introduces more dynamic character for expressive work. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage, a variable font, and a full OpenType feature set make it a functional choice for editorial, UI and identity projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Calleo as a modern sans-serif with two coherent but contrasting subfamilies — Calleo Sans and Calleo Flux — designed for versatility across minimalist and creative applications. With 36 styles plus a variable font, extended multilingual support, and advanced OpenType features, they position it as a flexible family suited to logos, headlines, UI, editorial and identity systems.

name: Grima
classification_notes: Grima blends unicase and monospaced traits in a display context, originating from flat brush lettering sketches with calligraphic nuances — placing it firmly in experimental display rather than any conventional text category.
summary: A bold unicase display typeface built across five widths and a variable font, drawing from flat brush lettering and monospaced grid logic.
description: Designed by Todor Georgiev and released by Fontfabric in 2023, Grima takes its conceptual cue from 'Unispace' thinking — merging unicase letterforms with the consistent rhythm of monospaced type. The family spans five widths from Narrow to UltraWide, with a variable font for smooth transitions across the range. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage, plus OpenType ligatures and localisation features, make it a practical choice for poster, packaging and branding work across multiple languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Grima as a bold contemporary display typeface inspired by experimental Unispace concepts that blend unicase and monospaced traits. It originated from pencil sketches of flat brush lettering with calligraphic nuances, and is designed for poster headlines, branding, packaging, editorial layouts and digital banners. Five width styles plus a variable font give designers expressive, technically precise display typography with multilingual support across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Gismo
classification_notes: A geometric display family built around four distinct corner treatments ranging from fully rounded to fully rectangular, placing it squarely in the display category with a geometric structure.
summary: A geometric display family in four corner-treatment styles, ranging from soft rounds to hard rectangles, released by Fontfabric with Ani Dimitrova in 2023.
description: Four static styles plus a variable font let designers slide between approachable rounded forms and angular technical precision within a single coherent family. The range suits headlines, packaging and branding work where tonal flexibility matters across a campaign. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage, alongside OpenType features including ligatures and contextual alternates, broadens its practical reach.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Gismo as a display family created in collaboration with Ani Dimitrova, exploring the full spectrum of geometric form through four styles — Round, Semi-round, Semi-rectangular and Rectangular — designed to take branding and editorial work from approachable warmth through to technical precision.

name: Muller Next
classification_notes: Muller Next is a geometric sans-serif with a large width axis spanning Compressed through Ultra Wide, consistent with the clean, neutrally structured letterforms typical of the geometric grotesque tradition.
summary: A large geometric sans-serif system spanning nine width variants and twelve weights, available as a variable font.
description: Spanning Compressed to Ultra Wide across twelve weights from Hairline to Fat, the family totals 218 styles with matching italics throughout. A variable font option covers the full range in two files, making it practical for responsive work. The scale is its main selling point, though the letterforms themselves are functional rather than distinctive.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric present Muller Next as a comprehensive geometric sans-serif family offering nine width variants from Compressed to Ultra Wide, twelve weights from Hairline to Fat, and a variable font option, amounting to 218 styles in total.

name: Drumnik
designer: Stopan, Plamen Motev, Stefan Yatanski, Mirela Belcheva
classification_notes: A vintage-inspired display serif drawing on Bulgarian hand-lettered Slavic calligraphy, with texture and calligraphic stroke contrast placing it firmly in the display serif category rather than a text serif tradition.
summary: A Bulgarian-rooted display serif released on the Day of the Slavonic Alphabet, offered in clean and hand-textured variants drawn from traditional Slavic calligraphy.
description: Designed by four collaborators including Bulgarian bookbinder Stopan, whose hand-lettered scripts provided the source material, Drumnik blends calligraphic Slavic letterforms with digital construction. It comes in two styles — a clean version and a richly textured one — giving designers a straightforward way to dial up or down the handcrafted quality. Over 640 glyphs cover Extended Latin and Cyrillic, with OpenType features including double-story ligatures and stylistic alternates for expressive display use.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Drumnik as a vintage-inspired display serif drawing from Bulgarian bookbinder Stopan's hand-lettered scripts, blending traditional Slavic calligraphy with modern digital forms. Released on 24 May — the Day of the Slavonic Alphabet, Bulgarian Enlightenment, and Culture — it is offered in clean and textured versions with over 640 glyphs supporting Extended Latin and Cyrillic, and is suited to posters, packaging, album art, branding, and cultural publications.

name: Audela
classification_notes: The foundry describes Audela as an Antiqua-rooted serif with sharp, wedge-shaped serifs and medium contrast letterforms that bridge traditional Antiqua elegance with contemporary precision, placing it squarely in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: A sharp-serifed Antiqua family begun as an intern project in 2018 and released in 2022 across seven weights with matching italics and variable fonts.
description: Designed by Léa Bruneau under the mentorship of Fontfabric type director Plamen Motev, Audela takes its name from the French au-delà and aims to push past conventional serif conventions with wedge-shaped serifs and flowing, medium-contrast letterforms. The family spans seven weights from Extralight to Black, each with an italic, plus separate variable Roman and variable Italic fonts. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage alongside a full OpenType feature set makes it serviceable for editorial, branding and book cover work across multiple markets.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Audela as a serif typeface designed for high-impact print and digital design, with sharp wedge-shaped serifs and expressive letterforms that connect traditional Antiqua elegance to contemporary precision. They highlight its 16 styles, advanced OpenType features including contextual alternates, ligatures and stylistic sets, and extended Latin and Cyrillic multilingual support as making it a versatile choice for brand identities, magazine layouts, editorial titles and web headers.

name: Gwen
classification_notes: Gwen is a high-contrast serif with two optical subfamilies — Display and Text — designed explicitly for editorial and headline use, placing it clearly in the display serif tradition with transitional influences visible in its stroke contrast.
summary: A high-contrast display serif from Fontfabric with separate Display and Text subfamilies, offered as a variable font covering seven weights.
description: Designed by Pavel Pavlov and Plamen Motev, Gwen splits into two optical subfamilies: Display, intended for headlines and posters, and Text, tuned for smaller body sizes. The family spans seven weights from Light to Black across both subfamilies, with a variable font also available. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage, alongside OpenType features including ligatures, stylistic alternates and old-style figures, make it serviceable for editorial and multilingual publishing work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Gwen as a high-contrast display serif crafted as a variable font system, with Display and Text subfamilies for headlines and smaller sizes respectively. They position it for editorial layouts, posters, packaging and landing pages, highlighting its strong typographic voice, extended Latin and Cyrillic support, and a comprehensive OpenType feature set including ligatures, alternates, fractions and variable font axes.

name: Ways
classification_notes: Ways blends grotesque structure with humanist detail — ink traps, wider apexes, and extra white space give it warmth alongside functional rigour. Fontfabric describe it as a mix of brutal and elegant details with humanist flair.
summary: A nine-weight humanist grotesque with ink traps and variable fonts, designed for legibility across signage, editorial and digital work.
description: Developed by Viktoria Usmanova and Plamen Motev at Fontfabric, Ways spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus two variable fonts and an icons set — 21 styles in total. Ink traps, wider apexes and considered spacing underpin its legibility at both large and small sizes. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage with a full suite of OpenType features makes it suited to international projects across branding, UI and environmental graphics.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Ways as a collaborative sans-serif built for versatility and clarity, with a unique blend of brutal and elegant details alongside humanist flair. Visual compensations, extra white space, wider apexes and moderate ink traps contribute to its distinct look and high legibility across all 21 styles, with multilingual support for Extended Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Fester
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Plamen Motev, Ventsislav Dzhokov
classification_notes: A semi-condensed grotesque with four-square geometry, sharp terminals and low contrast, placing it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition with a condensed, tech-forward character.
summary: A semi-condensed geometric grotesque from Fontfabric, built across eight weights with a variable font and Cyrillic support for tech branding and editorial work.
description: Released in March 2022, Fester was developed over several years by Svetoslav Simov, Plamen Motev and Ventsislav Dzhokov. Low contrast and sharp terminals give it a hard-edged, space-efficient quality suited to headlines, UI layouts and product packaging. The family ships with 16 styles, a companion variable font, and a 760-plus glyph set covering more than 130 languages across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Fester as a semi-condensed grotesque honed over several years before its March 2022 release, crafted to deliver clear, low-contrast letterforms in slick geometric shapes built for big messages in tight layouts. They position it for futuristic editorial, interface and branding work, noting its four-square geometry, sharp terminals and variable font support across 130-plus languages.

name: Code Next
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Mirela Belova, Stan Partalev
classification_notes: Clean, simplified geometric forms with a modern character, explicitly positioned as a geometric sans in the tradition of Futura and Avenir, with multi-script support and variable font axes.
summary: A geometric sans from Fontfabric evolved from their Code Pro family, offering ten weights with italics and two variable fonts across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Released in March 2021 as a successor to Code Pro, it was drawn by Svetoslav Simov, Mirela Belova and Stan Partalev. Twenty static styles plus two variable fonts cover a wide weight range from Hairline to Black, with an extensive OpenType feature set including small caps, old-style figures and stylistic alternates. Broad multi-script support across Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek makes it a workable choice for international branding and UI projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Code Next as an evolution of their Code Pro family, designed for branding, advertising, UI/UX, posters, editorial and packaging. They highlight a distinctive geometric 'twist', advanced multi-script support covering Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and two variable fonts as markers of its forward-thinking design philosophy.

name: Mont Blanc
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans-serif by the foundry, with elevated x-height and text-optimised spacing derived from the geometric Mont family.
summary: A text-optimised geometric sans derived from Fontfabric's Mont, adding elevated x-height and tighter spacing across eight weights with matching italics and variable fonts.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov and Viktoria Usmanova, Mont Blanc takes the geometric character of the Mont family and refines it for smaller sizes, with improved spacing and legibility for body copy. The family runs to 16 static styles plus two variable fonts covering the full weight range from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. Extended Latin, Greek and Cyrillic coverage with over 700 glyphs makes it serviceable across multilingual editorial, UI and branding projects.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Mont Blanc as a geometric sans-serif designed for text-heavy layouts as well as headlines, built on the success of their Mont family. Prioritising clarity at smaller sizes through an elevated x-height, tighter spacing and improved legibility, it is intended to work equally well for magazine articles, UI text, branding, signage and packaging.

name: Alkes
classification_notes: Fontfabric describe it as a humanist serif, with generous x-height, open counters and subtle angled stress across a broad weight range, balancing digital precision with analogue warmth.
summary: A humanist serif originating from Kaja Słojewska's graduation project at the University of Reading, refined into a 14-style family covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Developed from a University of Reading graduation project and later refined with Plamen Motev and Nikolay Petroussenko at Fontfabric, it draws on cosmic naming and a humanist structure defined by generous x-height, open counters and angled stress. The family runs seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and carries an extensive OpenType feature set including small caps, old-style figures, contextual alternates and fractions. Suited to editorial, publishing and multilingual branding work.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Alkes as a humanist serif named after a star and inspired by the cosmos, designed to bring together Latin, Cyrillic and Greek in a single coherent system. They position it for editorial, publishing, branding, headlines and long-form text, noting its generous x-height, wide spacing and open counters as key to its legibility across both body copy and display settings.

name: Pixer
classification_notes: Constructed entirely from square pixels in the tradition of early bitmap fonts, Pixer is firmly a display typeface evoking 1970s arcade game aesthetics rather than any conventional serif or sans-serif classification.
summary: A single-weight pixel display font by Nikolay Petroussenko, built from a strict grid of squares in the manner of 1970s arcade game bitmap lettering.
description: Designed in 2020 and offered free by Fontfabric, Pixer draws its structure from the bitmap fonts of early video game hardware, referencing titles like Space Invaders as a touchstone. The all-pixel construction is optimised to read cleanly at both low and high screen resolutions, and OpenType standard ligatures are included. Suited to gaming interfaces, retro-themed branding and digital advertising that calls for an overtly nostalgic register.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Pixer as a display pixel font inspired by the early digital aesthetics of the 1970s and the golden age of arcade video games such as Space Invaders. Built entirely from pixels and optimised for both low- and high-resolution screens, the font is offered free and targets designers and gamers who want to evoke classic bitmap typography in modern digital applications.

name: Nexa
classification_notes: Nexa is explicitly circle-based with clean geometric forms and low contrast, placing it squarely in the geometric sans-serif tradition alongside faces like Gotham and Avenir.
summary: A circle-based geometric sans from Fontfabric, spanning 36 styles across a main display subfamily and a text-optimised variant with looser spacing.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov and released in 2012, Nexa built its reputation on clean geometric construction and well-optimised kerning suited to branding, UI and advertising. The family runs nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a dedicated Nexa Text subfamily widens the internal spacing for longer-form reading. OpenType features include small caps, tabular figures, fractions and a stylistic set offering squared stroke alternates for display use, with Cyrillic and Extended Latin covering over 130 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Nexa as a modern geometric sans-serif fundamentally based on a circle, designed for a simple, recognisable and contemporary appearance. Originally a bestseller, it has been significantly updated to improve versatility and legibility, with the addition of the Nexa Text subfamily optimised for longer writing, an ExtraLight weight, expanded language support, and richer OpenType functionality including localised Cyrillic variants for Azeri, Kazakh, Turkish and Bulgarian.

name: The Ultimate Bundle
classification_notes: A curated bundle of 97 display font styles spanning geometric, stencil, outline, inline, decorative and expressive designs; no single classification applies, but display is the dominant intent across all included faces.
summary: A curated bundle of 97 display styles drawn from Fontfabric's back catalogue, sold as a single discounted package for logo and headline work.
description: Spanning over 40 named typefaces, the collection gathers geometric, stencil, outline, inline and decorative styles produced during Fontfabric's first era of development, many by founder Svet Simov. Weight coverage runs from Thin to Black across individual families, and OpenType feature support varies by face. The bundle is pitched at branding, packaging and editorial projects where visual range and price per style are the primary concerns.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe The Ultimate Font Bundle as a curated milestone representing years of creative craftsmanship, bringing together iconic and versatile display typefaces—many by founder Svet Simov—into a single package. They position it as a passport to typographic possibilities for designers working on logos, headlines and comprehensive branding projects, with 97 styles, broad multilingual support and varying OpenType features across the included fonts.

name: 36 Days
designer: Plamen Motev, Mirela Belova, Nikolay Petroussenko, Laurene Girbal, Stan Partalev
classification_notes: A single-weight experimental display font drawing on optical art, copperplate, blackletter and sign painting traditions, designed purely for expressive headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A free single-weight display font from Fontfabric, born from the 36 Days of Type challenge and drawing on optical art, blackletter and sign painting traditions.
description: Created by a team at Fontfabric and released as a free download in 2019, the design pulls from a wide range of visual sources including the optical art of Victor Vasarely, Penrose's impossible objects, copperplate script, blackletter and Eastern dry brush technique. It ships as a single Regular style with extended Latin support and OpenType features, aimed at posters, social media graphics and experimental headline work. The eclectic reference points give it a handcrafted, one-off quality that suits illustrative and editorial contexts over sustained reading.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe 36 Days as a fully realised display font inspired by the global 36 Days of Type challenge, offered free to give back to the creative community. Drawing on optical art pioneers like Victor Vasarely and Roger Penrose alongside copperplate, blackletter, sign painting and Eastern brush techniques, it is intended for expressive posters, headlines, social media graphics and experimental typography projects.

name: Intro
classification_notes: Directly inspired by early 20th-century geometric sans-serif forms, drawing on Futura, Avant Garde and DIN, with triangles, circles and squares as its structural basis and humanist details softening the italics.
summary: A geometric sans from Fontfabric spanning 72 styles across standard and condensed widths, with inline and alternate variants for editorial and branding work.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov and released in 2012, Intro draws its structure from Futura, Avant Garde and DIN, combining pure geometric forms with humanist inflections that are most visible in the flowing italics. The family runs from Thin to Black in both standard and condensed widths, with obliques, true italics, alternate characters and inline cuts at the heavy end. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage alongside OpenType features including small caps, old-style figures and case-sensitive forms make it a practical workhorse across editorial, packaging, UI and motion graphics.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Intro as a geometric sans inspired by early 20th-century forms, combining triangles, circles and squares with subtle humanist details. Designed by Svetoslav Simov with reference to DIN, Futura and Avant Garde, the family spans 72 styles with advanced OpenType features and multilingual support, suited to editorial design, branding, web, motion graphics and packaging.

name: Panton Rust
designer: Plamen Motev, Stan Partalev, Ventsislav Dzhokov
classification_notes: A display typeface built around layered tactile effects — shadow, grunge, inline and fill variants — designed explicitly for headlines, packaging and branding rather than running text.
summary: A 72-style textured display family from Fontfabric, built on the Panton skeleton and layered with grunge, shadow, inline and fill variants for vintage and crafted aesthetics.
description: Companion to the original Panton sans-serif, this family extends that geometric foundation into heavily textured territory across five weights from SemiBold to Heavy. Each weight ships with multiple texture layers — Base, Shadow, Grunge, Grunge Shadow, Grunge Fill and Inline — designed to be stacked for multicolour effects. A Script subset mirrors the weight range with its own texture treatments, and the whole family covers Extended Latin and Cyrillic for over 130 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Panton Rust as a textured display typeface designed by Plamen Motev, Stan Partalev and Ventsislav Dzhokov, conceived as a companion to the original Panton family. They position it for vintage and crafted creative projects, offering 72 fonts across five weights with an array of tactile textures including shadow, grunge, inline and fill variants, supporting Extended Latin and Cyrillic for over 130 languages.

name: Mozer
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Ani Dimitrova, Mirela Belova, Nikolay Petroussenko
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque sans-serif; the semi-condensed proportions, generous x-height, and moderate contrast are characteristic of the neo-grotesque tradition in the vein of Bureau Grot or URW DIN.
summary: A semi-condensed neo-grotesque from Fontfabric with a generous x-height and subtle ink traps, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek across 16 styles.
description: Released in 2019 by Fontfabric, Mozer is built for economical setting with its moderately narrow proportions and carefully calibrated forms that hold up in both headlines and extended text. Its 825-glyph set spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, giving it reach across more than 100 languages. The full OpenType feature set includes stylistic alternates, ligatures, contextual alternates and a complete range of figure styles.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Mozer as a semi-condensed neo-grotesque sans-serif designed for legibility and contemporary appeal, with a generous x-height, economical width and subtle ink traps. They position it as versatile across print and digital environments, noting its 16 styles, extensive OpenType features and multilingual support covering over 100 languages via extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.

name: Intro Script
classification_notes: A flowing, multi-weight script with layered decorative components derived from the Intro Rust sans family, designed for display and branding use rather than body text.
summary: A warm, layerable script family with eight weights and a set of decorative goodies — words, banners and patterns — spun off from the Intro Rust type system.
description: Developed by Ventsislav Dzhokov and Plamen Motev at Fontfabric, the family extends the grunge-inflected Intro Rust sans into flowing script territory across eight weights from Thin to Black. Three additional Goodies styles — pre-built words, banners and patterns — give designers quick decorative assets for packaging, posters and social media. OpenType features include contextual alternates, ligatures and positional variations, with coverage across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Intro Script as emerging from the Intro Rust family, crafted by Ventsislav Dzhokov, extending the grunge-infused sans into a warm, flowing script system complete with layered decorative elements — words, patterns and banners — billed as a toolkit for eye-catching, handcrafted typographic design across branding, packaging, posters and social media.

name: Noah
designer: Svetoslav Simov, Radomir Tinkov, Stan Partalev
classification_notes: Blends geometric construction with grotesque detailing, including humanistic terminals and OpenType alternates such as a binocular 'g'. Extended x-height and vertical stress are characteristic of the geometric sans genre.
summary: A geometric sans-serif from Fontfabric spanning 72 styles across four subfamilies tuned for text, display and grotesque applications.
description: Released in 2019 by Svetoslav Simov, Radomir Tinkov and Stan Partalev, Noah is organised into four sub-families — Noah, Noah Grotesque, Noah Head and Noah Text — each running from Thin to Black with true italics throughout. The extended x-height and moderate contrast make it adaptable across screen and print, while OpenType alternates including a binocular 'g' and geometric 'a' add stylistic range. Latin Extended and Cyrillic coverage spans more than 130 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Noah as a geometric sans-serif that blends the best of geometric and grotesque traditions, with an extended x-height, vertical stress, moderate contrast and humanistic terminals. The 72-style family offers advanced OpenType features including small caps, alternates and ligatures, with support for over 130 languages across Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Singel
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as neoclassical, with semi-condensed proportions, rationalist modulated stroke and crisp serifs placing it firmly in the classical roman tradition with a contemporary refinement.
summary: A neoclassical serif with semi-condensed proportions, designed for upscale branding and editorial work with a full set of small capitals and true italics.
description: Designed by Jacklina Jekova and Nikolay Petroussenko for Fontfabric, Singel draws on rationalist modulated strokes and crisp serifs to produce a restrained, elegant roman suited to high-fashion branding and luxury packaging. The family spans five weights from Light to Bold, each with a true italic, giving ten styles in total. OpenType features include small capitals, tabular numerals, fractions and case-sensitive forms, with broad multilingual Latin support.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Singel as a neoclassical serif with semi-condensed proportions and a rationalist modulated stroke that combines timeless sophistication with modern usability. Designed for luxurious visual identities, it offers a full set of small capitals, true italics, and comprehensive OpenType features, making it suitable for high-fashion branding, luxury product packaging, editorial layouts and digital applications.

name: Squad
classification_notes: Squad is described as inspired by Adrian Frutiger and blends humanist heritage with contemporary simplicity; its semi-condensed proportions and balanced rhythm align it with the humanist sans-serif tradition.
summary: A semi-condensed humanist sans by Svetoslav Simov, released in 2018 and drawing on the Frutiger tradition for broad multilingual use.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov at Fontfabric, Squad takes its cues from Adrian Frutiger's approach to legibility and balanced rhythm, arriving in 18 styles across nine weights with matching italics. Its semi-condensed proportions suit both dense body text and large headlines, and the family covers Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek for over 130 languages. OpenType features include tabular numerals, fractions, discretionary ligatures and case sensitivity.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Squad as a meticulously crafted sans serif inspired by Adrian Frutiger's perfectionist style, blending humanist heritage with contemporary simplicity and functional legibility. Released in August 2018 with 18 styles and support for over 130 languages across Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, it is positioned for corporate branding, editorial design, UI, signage, packaging and multilingual content.

name: Slovic
classification_notes: Slovic is a display family rooted in Old Church Slavonic scripts such as Ustav and Vyaz, progressing stylistically through historic, serif, slab, semi-serif and sans-serif forms, making it primarily a display type system with historical and decorative intent.
summary: A Cyrillic display family by Plamen Motev that traces a stylistic arc from Old Church Slavonic scripts through to a clean sans-serif, all within one family.
description: Drawing on Ustav (Cyrillic uncial) and Vyaz (ornate lettering), the six-style family moves through historic, serif, slab, semi-serif and sans-serif forms, with a variable font tying the progression together. It covers Extended Latin and Cyrillic, with OpenType features and stylistic sets suited to heritage branding, book covers and cultural event material. The concept of a single family spanning such a wide stylistic range is unusual and gives designers a built-in contrast system rooted in Slavic typographic history.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Slovic as a journey through Cyrillic typography, inspired by historical Old Church Slavonic scripts. They highlight its defining feature as a stylistic progression from historic uncial forms through classic serif, slab, semi-serif and sans-serif styles, all unified in one family and extended by a variable font. They recommend it for projects requiring historical resonance and expressive flexibility.

name: Panton
classification_notes: Panton draws on classic grotesque traditions while applying softened geometric forms throughout, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category with humanist warmth around the terminals.
summary: A softened geometric sans from Fontfabric spanning 54 styles across standard, narrow and icon subfamilies, with broad multilingual support.
description: Designed by Ivan Petrov and Svetoslav Simov and released in 2015, Panton applies gently softened geometric forms across ten weights from Thin to Fat, each with a matching italic. The Narrow subfamily mirrors nine of those weights for tighter settings, and a suite of 16 icon fonts rounds out the family. Version 3.0 added Bulgarian localisation, tabular figures and case-sensitive punctuation, making it a practical choice for branding, editorial and screen work across multiple languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Panton as a sans-serif typeface inspired by classic grotesques but developed into its own style through perfectly softened geometric forms, meticulous kerning and optimised legibility for both web and print. The complete family spans 54 styles including the Narrow subfamily and 16 bonus icon sets, with advanced OpenType features and multilingual support added in the 3.0 update.

name: Gilam
classification_notes: Inspired by the DIN neo-grotesque tradition with semi-condensed proportions, geometric clarity and sharp edge details; Fontfabric explicitly positions it within the DIN lineage while adding warmer terminal treatments.
summary: A DIN-influenced neo-grotesque with semi-condensed proportions, sharp edge details and broad multilingual coverage across 18 styles.
description: Designed by Plamen Motev and Ivan Petrov and released in 2018, Gilam draws on the DIN grotesque tradition while adding its own details: sharp cuts on the W and M, outward-facing terminals and a prominent x-height that aid readability at screen and print sizes. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek scripts. Suited to editorial layouts, UI work, packaging and signage where a controlled, slightly condensed sans is required.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Gilam as a neo-grotesque inspired by the DIN legacy, blending semi-condensed proportions and geometric clarity with warmer details such as sharp edges on the W and M and outward-cut terminals. The name means 'joy of people', and the family was built to reach a wide audience across editorial, branding, motion graphics, UI, packaging and signage. Eighteen styles with advanced OpenType features and support for Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek make it a versatile choice for multilingual projects.

name: Colus
classification_notes: A single-weight display serif with sharp, carved stone and wood letterform inspiration, placing it firmly in the display category rather than a text-optimised serif classification.
summary: A single-style display serif by Stan Partalev, inspired by carved stone and wood letterforms, released free by Fontfabric in 2018.
description: Sharp serif finishes and a classical structure give this face a weighty, inscriptional feel suited to headlines, posters and logotypes. It covers extended Latin and Cyrillic alphabets and includes OpenType features such as lining and old-style figures and stylistic alternates. The single weight limits its versatility but within its range it holds up well for brand identity and editorial title use.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Colus as the first professional typeface by Stan Partalev, inspired by carved stone and wood letterforms. They position it as a free display serif with a classic, mature aesthetic suited to headlines, signage and logos, with full extended Latin and Cyrillic support and OpenType features including lining and old-style figures, proper localizations and stylistic alternates.

name: Zing Rust
classification_notes: Zing Rust is a layered display family built from interchangeable base, fill, shadow, grunge, halftone and line components, designed for chromatic and retro-craft poster lettering rather than running text.
summary: A vast layered display system of 521 styles spanning serif, sans and script cuts with interchangeable grunge, halftone, diagonal and shadow layers for chromatic poster lettering.
description: The family splits into four sub-families: Zing Rust (serif-inflected display), Zing Sans Rust, Zing Script Rust and Zing Goodies (icon and banner elements). Each comes in multiple weights from Thin to Bold, with base layers combinable with grunge, halftone A and B, diagonal, horizontal line and shadow variants numbered one through five. The result is a toolkit aimed at vintage signage, food-and-beverage branding and retro editorial work where layered chromatic type is required.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric present Zing Rust as a comprehensive layered type system encompassing 521 font styles across serif display, sans and script subfamilies, supplemented by decorative goodies including bakery icons, BBQ icons, banners and words, all designed to be combined for richly textured, retro-inspired lettering compositions.

name: Averta
classification_notes: Averta is described by Fontfabric as a geometric sans-serif, combining monolinear geometric forms with humanist warmth derived from early European grotesques and Anglo-American gothics.
summary: A geometric sans-serif designed by Kostas Bartsokas in 2015, blending monolinear geometry with humanist detail across a large family spanning narrow, condensed and normal widths.
description: Built on the clarity of early European grotesques and Anglo-American gothics, Averta offers eight weights with true italics in three width variants, plus variable font styles. Its Cyrillic extension received a Granshan Award in 2017, and the family covers Latin, Greek, Vietnamese and Cyrillic with a full OpenType feature set including small caps, old-style figures and stylistic alternates. The breadth of styles and widths makes it a practical choice for branding, editorial and UI work requiring consistent cross-media typography.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Averta as born from the clarity of early European grotesques and the charm of Anglo-American gothics, designed by Kostas Bartsokas in 2015. They position it as a geometric sans-serif balancing geometric rigour with humanist warmth, suited to logos, editorial layouts, UI design, signage and packaging, and note that its Cyrillic script earned a Granshan Award in 2017.

name: Uni Neue
classification_notes: Uni Neue has a clear geometric construction with subtly rounded corners, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition. Its proportions and refined details align it with modernised geometric grotesques suitable for both display and text use.
summary: A modernised redesign of Fontfabric's Uni Sans, adding updated proportions, refined widths and subtly rounded corners across 14 styles.
description: A geometric sans-serif with softened corners, Uni Neue revisits the popular Uni Sans with improved spacing, weight distribution and overall refinement. Fourteen styles span Thin through Black with matching italics, and the family covers Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, making it suitable for international projects. Manual hinting is included for reliable rendering on screen and in web contexts.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Uni Neue as a sophisticated, modernised redesign of their highly popular Uni Sans family, crafted with updated proportions, improved widths and thicknesses, and carefully refined details. They position it as a contemporary sans-serif with a geometric backbone and subtle rounded corners, suited to TV and screen graphics, editorial publishing, branding and packaging. First published in February 2017 and last updated in November 2024, it offers 14 styles with Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek support, plus manual hinting for crisp cross-platform performance.

name: Cheque
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display typeface by the foundry, drawn from blackboard lettering with sharp geometric edges and a vintage-retro character. Its headline-focused design and limited weight range confirm a display classification.
summary: A two-weight display face by Mirela Belova, blending geometric construction with blackboard-lettering influence for a vintage-inflected headline style.
description: Designed by Mirela Belova as a student project at Fontfabric and released in 2017, the family spans just two cuts, Regular and Black, both freely available for personal and commercial use. Sharp geometric edges sit alongside a retro, hand-drawn quality drawn from blackboard scripts, making it a useful choice for posters, album covers and editorial headlines. Multilingual support covers extended Latin and Cyrillic, with OpenType features included.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Cheque as a display typeface created in 2017 by Mirela Belova during her time as a student at the foundry. Inspired by blackboard-style lettering and geometric form, the design carries a vintage-classic feel, and both the Regular and Black weights are free to download for personal and commercial use.

name: Madelyn
classification_notes: A handwritten brush script with natural calligraphic flow, decorative doodle variants and fill styles, placing it firmly in the casual brush script category.
summary: A casual brush script with decorative doodle and fill variants, designed for branding, packaging and expressive editorial use.
description: Designed by Svetoslav Simov and Jacklina Jekova and released in 2016, Madelyn draws on natural brush calligraphy to produce a handwritten script with realistic ligatures and stylistic alternates. The family extends beyond a plain script with Fill and Doodles variants, giving designers ready-made decorative options for greeting cards, product labels and social media. OpenType features and coverage of Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts add practical versatility to what is otherwise a straightforwardly expressive typeface.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Madelyn as a handwritten script typeface inspired by fluid natural brush calligraphy, offering a casual yet elegant feel. Released in November 2016, the four-style family includes Regular, Fill, Doodles and Doodles Fill cuts, and is recommended for branding, invitations, packaging, social media and editorial titles. OpenType features include stylistic alternates, discretionary alternates, ligatures and localisation, with multilingual support across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Mixa
classification_notes: Mixa is built on a neo-grotesque sans-serif structure, but its lowercase letters incorporate a slanted script quality, creating a dual personality between all-caps clarity and expressive handwritten forms.
summary: A neo-grotesque with a slanted script character built into the lowercase, creating a dual all-caps/mixed-case personality across eight weights.
description: Designed by Ani Dimitrova and released in 2016, Mixa uses a neo-grotesque skeleton as its base while infusing the lowercase with a subtle slanted script quality, so all-caps settings read clean and clinical while mixed-case reveals a handwritten warmth. The eight weights from Thin to Black give it range across branding, packaging and editorial contexts. OpenType features include ligatures, stylistic alternates and discretionary ligatures, with coverage across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Fontfabric describe Mixa as a sans-serif with a dual personality: a crisp all-caps structure that transforms into a smooth handwritten script in the lowercase. Designed by Ani Dimitrova, it is positioned for branding, headlines, packaging, editorial and web UI, with eight styles and support for Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
